findings.topic: Soar decision cycle as the agent execution loop: parallel knowledge retrieval, serialized commit
findings.summary: Soar Manual ch.2: execution proceeds in decision cycles of 5 phases — Input (sensory data into working memory), Proposal (productions fire AND retract in parallel across repeated elaboration cycles until quiescence: state elaboration, operator proposal, operator comparison; all i-supported), Decision (architectural selection of exactly ONE operator via preference semantics, or an impasse is detected and a new substate created), Application (o-supported operator-application productions fire to quiescence), Output (commands sent to environment). Soar About page states the core principle: 'All decisions are made through the combination of relevant knowledge at run-time... Decisions are never precompiled into uninterruptible sequences.' Preference semantics (acceptable/reject/better/worse/best/worst/require/prohibit) are evaluated architecturally to pick the single operator.
findings.designImplication: The kernel's per-agent scheduler quantum should be a decision cycle, not a timeslice: an unbounded-parallelism match/retrieve phase (rule engines, vector lookups, tool-result ingestion can all run concurrently) followed by a single serialized commit point where exactly one action/operator is chosen. Preemption and interrupt delivery land only at cycle boundaries — never mid-commit — which gives agents Soar's 'never uninterruptible' guarantee for free. Preference-style conflict resolution (a small algebra of better/worse/require/prohibit) belongs in the kernel as the default arbitration mechanism between competing proposed actions; the proposal generators (LLM calls, rules) stay userspace-pluggable.

findings.topic: Soar working memory: typed graph of identifier-attribute-value triples with reachability-based garbage collection
findings.summary: Soar Manual ch.2: working memory holds WMEs, each an identifier-attribute-value triple ('B1 is a block', 'B1 is named A'); all WMEs sharing an identifier form an object; values may be identifiers of other objects, making WM a single connected directed graph rooted at states. Critically: 'All objects in working memory must be linked to a state, either directly or indirectly... Objects that are not linked to a state will be automatically removed from working memory by the Soar architecture.' Persistence is dual: i-supported WMEs (derived inferences) retract automatically the instant their justifying conditions stop matching; o-supported WMEs (operator effects) persist until explicitly removed.
findings.designImplication: Ship working memory as a kernel-managed graph store (triples/property-graph), not a flat key-value blob, with root-anchored reachability GC: anything not reachable from a live agent goal/state is reclaimed automatically — agents never free memory manually. The i-support/o-support split maps to two kernel persistence classes: 'derived/memoized' facts that auto-invalidate when their dependencies change (kernel tracks the justification) vs 'committed' facts that survive until an explicit action removes them. This dependency-tracked invalidation should be a kernel default, since userspace agents (especially LLM-driven ones) reliably forget to clean up stale derived beliefs.

findings.topic: Soar impasses and automatic subgoaling: knowledge failure as an architectural trap mechanism
findings.summary: Soar Manual ch.2 defines four impasse types detected architecturally when the decision phase cannot select an operator: tie (preferences do not distinguish two or more acceptable operators), conflict (contradictory better/worse preferences, e.g. A>B and B>A), constraint-failure (more than one required value, or require+prohibit on the same value), and no-change (no new operator selected). On impasse the architecture automatically creates a new substate whose goal is to resolve the impasse; problem solving recurses in the substate (possibly impassing again, creating a goal stack). Results returned to the superstate resolve the impasse and trigger substate removal, governed by the Goal Dependency Set (GDS) which removes substates whose dependent WMEs changed.
findings.designImplication: Make 'agent cannot decide' a first-class kernel trap, exactly like a page fault: when arbitration yields tie/conflict/constraint-failure/no-change, the kernel automatically spawns a child reasoning context (substate) with the impasse type and contending options as its input, rather than returning an error. For an LLM-native OS this is the natural architectural slot for escalation policies: impasse handler = invoke a (possibly larger/slower) model, ask another agent, or query memory — pluggable in userspace, but impasse detection, substate stack management, and GDS-style automatic teardown of invalidated sub-contexts are kernel-level. This converts meta-reasoning from an application pattern into an OS service.

findings.topic: Soar chunking: automatic compilation of substate problem-solving traces into new procedural rules
findings.summary: Soar Manual ch.2: when an impasse is resolved, chunking 'attempts to create a new production, called a chunk. The conditions of the chunk are the elements of the state that (through some chain of production firings) allowed the impasse to be resolved; the action... is the working memory element or preference that resolved the impasse.' Conditions/actions are variablized for generalization, so future similar situations fire the chunk directly, 'preempting the need for a substate and repeated deliberate problem solving.' Chunks are permanent generalized forms of justifications (which are instantiated and vanish when conditions stop matching); both are built by the same result-dependency-trace mechanism. Soar 9.6.5 (current release per homepage) added a 'test-literalization' operator for finer control over chunking generalization. Soar About page: learning expanded from single-mechanism chunking to four mechanisms — chunking, reinforcement learning, episodic learning, semantic learning.
findings.designImplication: Self-improvement as a native OS service has a 40-year-old blueprint: a kernel-level skill compiler that watches sub-context (impasse-resolution / delegated-LLM-call) traces and compiles them into cached, parameterized procedures so the expensive deliberation path is skipped next time. Two hard requirements transfer directly: (1) compilation must be based on a dependency trace of what inputs actually determined the result (Soar's backtracing), not the whole context — otherwise compiled skills overfit; (2) generalization aggressiveness must be tunable per-skill (Soar's test-literalization precedent), i.e. the compiler is a kernel default but its generalization policy is pluggable. Plan for the LLM analog: traces are natural-language reasoning chains, so the 'backtrace' needs explicit provenance capture at tool-call/memory-read boundaries, which the kernel can record automatically.

findings.topic: Soar semantic memory: SQLite-backed long-term declarative store with pluggable activation (recency, frequency, base-level, spreading)
findings.summary: Soar Manual ch.6 (SMem): long-term identifiers (LTIs, e.g. @5) live only in semantic memory; retrieval instantiates fresh short-term copies in WM so edits never silently corrupt the store. Storage is SQLite, in-memory or on-disk, with lazy-commit default on. Cue-based retrieval is exact-match over attribute-value cues plus modifiers (prohibit, neg-query, math-query with less/greater/max/min). Ties are broken by activation: default mode is recency; alternatives are frequency, base-level activation via the Petrov approximation BLA = log[Σ_{i=1..k} t_i^{-d} + (n-k)(t_n^{1-d} - t_k^{1-d}) / ((1-d)(t_n - t_k))] with k hard-coded to 10 stored boosts, and spreading activation (additive on top of BLA, requires it) with spreading-continue-probability default 0.9 (multiplicative with depth: grandchild gets 0.81), spreading-limit default 300 LTIs per source, spreading-depth-limit default 10. Performance: queries carry up to 40% overhead vs rete matching but 'sub-millisecond retrieval time for a large class of queries, even in very large stores (millions of nodes/edges)'; storage 'far less than 1KB per stored WME' past ~1000-WME overhead.
findings.designImplication: Concrete validation that a default persistent semantic tier can be an embedded kernel service, not a remote database: an embedded store (SQLite/successor) with activation-ranked cue retrieval scales to millions of nodes at sub-ms latency and <1KB/fact. Design choices to copy: (a) activation mode is a pluggable policy enum (recency default, frequency, base-level, +spreading) on a fixed kernel retrieval API; (b) copy-on-retrieve semantics (LTI vs STI split) so working memory edits are isolated from the long-term store until an explicit store/commit syscall; (c) bounded spreading (probability 0.9, fan-out 300, depth 10) shows context-priming can be made worst-case-bounded and therefore safe to run inside a kernel path. The 40%-over-rete and 8k-page numbers give initial capacity-planning baselines for the docs.

findings.topic: Soar episodic memory: automatic flight-recorder journaling with cue-based time-travel retrieval and replay
findings.summary: Soar Manual ch.7 (EpMem): 'Episodic memory records new episodes without deliberate action/consideration by the agent' — each episode snapshots the entire top-state of WM, triggered by default on new output-link augmentations, at end of each decision cycle phase; an exclusions parameter omits subtrees (epmem/smem structures excluded by default to prevent recording memory retrievals recursively). Episodes get monotonically increasing integer times starting at 1. Retrieval: cue-based search is 'conceptually... a nearest-neighbor search' scoring candidates by (balance)·(cardinality) + (1-balance)·(activation of matching leaf WMEs), with optional exact graph-match as a second stage (worst-case combinatorial); temporal modifiers before/after/prohibit; absolute retrieval by time; relative next/previous commands let agents 'play forward' episodes. Metadata returned includes match-score, normalized-match-score (confidence proxy), memory-id and present-id (relative age). 'The current episodic memory implementation does not implement any episodic store dynamics, such as forgetting' — worst-case cue search is linear in episodes. SQLite-backed; default page-size 8192 bytes chosen from a benchmark of 10 million decisions (1 episode/decision) on a 2.8GHz Core i7.
findings.designImplication: Ship episodic memory as a kernel-level automatic journal (flight recorder) of agent state — capture is architectural, never an agent responsibility, with kernel-enforced default exclusion lists to stop self-referential recording of the memory system itself. The retrieval API to standardize: cue-match scored by tunable cardinality-vs-activation balance, temporal range operators, and next/previous replay cursors (this is the substrate for agent self-debugging, audit, and learning-from-experience). Two gaps the OS must fix that Soar punts on: no forgetting (a greenfield kernel needs default compaction/summarization tiers for unbounded journals) and linear-scan worst case (plan secondary indexes/embedding indexes as pluggable accelerators over the canonical journal).

findings.topic: ACT-R modules and buffers: bounded one-chunk context registers as the only inter-module interface
findings.summary: ACT-R About page: main components are modules (perceptual-motor: visual, manual; memory: declarative chunks and procedural productions), buffers, and a pattern matcher. 'ACT-R accesses its modules (except for the procedural-memory module) through buffers. For each module, a dedicated buffer serves as the interface... The contents of the buffers at a given moment in time represents the state of ACT-R at that moment.' Reference manual (p./line ~946): a buffer is used 'to relay requests for actions to its module, to query its module about the module's state, to hold one chunk which is visible to all other modules.' The pattern matcher fires exactly one production per cycle; default production action time :dat = 0.05s (50ms) ('generally that value is not changed'). Modules run asynchronously behind their buffers; production firing is the serial bottleneck.
findings.designImplication: Adopt buffers as the kernel ABI between an agent's core loop and every subsystem: a fixed set of named, bounded context registers (goal, retrieval, perception, tool-result...), each holding exactly one typed record, with all heavy machinery (vector search, tool execution, LLM inference) running as asynchronous module services behind a request/query/result protocol — the device-driver model for cognition. For LLM agents this becomes the principled answer to context management: the kernel materializes the prompt from the register set (declared, inspectable, bounded) rather than letting agents append unboundedly to a context blob. Register count/width should be a kernel default tuned for context-window economics (ACT-R's 1-chunk width is calibrated to human cognition, so make width configurable per-register, but the registers-not-heap contract itself is kernel-level and non-negotiable).

findings.topic: ACT-R activation and base-level learning: power-law recency-frequency decay as a retrieval-ranking and eviction policy
findings.summary: ACT-R 7 reference manual, declarative module: chunk activation Ai = Bi + Si + Pi + ε (base-level + spreading + partial-match + noise); the matching chunk with highest activation above retrieval threshold :rt (default 0.0) is retrieved, else failure. Base-level learning: Bi = ln(Σ_{j=1..n} t_j^{-d}) + βi where t_j is time since jth presentation and d is decay (:bll); 'The recommended value for :bll is .5, and it is one of the few parameters which have a strong recommended value.' Optimized-learning approximation avoids storing full history: Bi = ln(n/(1-d)) - d·ln(L) + βi (n presentations, L lifetime); a hybrid keeps the k most recent true references (the same form Soar adopted as the Petrov approximation with k=10). Retrieval latency: RT = F·e^(-f·Ai) seconds, and failure time RT = F·e^(-f·τ); latency factor F default 1.0, exponent f default 1.0 — lower activation means measurably slower retrieval.
findings.designImplication: The default memory-tier ranking/eviction policy for an agent OS should be base-level activation, i.e. log of power-law-decayed access history with d=0.5 — not LRU and not plain frequency: it is the single best-validated number in 50 years of cognitive modeling and both major architectures converged on it (Soar imported it verbatim). Implement at kernel level using the optimized/hybrid approximation (O(1) state per item: count, creation time, last-k timestamps with k≈10) so it costs no more than LRU bookkeeping. The latency equation RT = F·e^(-f·A) doubles as a cost model: the kernel scheduler can price a memory retrieval before issuing it and decide between waiting, re-deriving, or escalating — F and f become pluggable per-backend calibration constants (vector DB vs local cache vs remote API).

findings.topic: ACT-R spreading activation, fan effect, partial matching, and noise: context-primed, interference-aware, stochastic retrieval
findings.summary: ACT-R 7 reference manual: spreading activation Si = Σ_k Σ_j Wkj·Sji, summing over buffers k holding chunks with non-zero source activation; the chunks j in those buffer chunks' slots are the activation sources; Wkj = buffer source activation divided by number of sources. Strength of association Sji = S - ln(fan_ji), where fan is how many chunks j is connected to and S is :mas — meaning the more facts an item participates in, the weaker each individual association (the fan effect). Partial matching Pi (enabled by :mp mismatch penalty) lets near-miss chunks be retrieved at an activation penalty. Noise ε has transient and permanent components; 'Recommended values for the noise parameter are in the range [.2,.8]' (:ans, default nil/off). Off by default: :mas default nil (no spreading), :mp default nil (exact match only).
findings.designImplication: Retrieval in the default memory service should be biased by what is currently in the agent's context registers (spreading from buffer contents), so 'related-to-what-I'm-doing-now' beats 'globally popular' — this is the cognitive-architecture form of contextual RAG, and Wkj source-splitting gives a principled budget: total priming influence is fixed, divided among current context items. The fan effect (Sji = S - ln(fan)) is a warning to encode in the kernel's memory-shaping defaults: hub entities with thousands of links degrade retrieval precision, so the store should favor specific composite keys/chunks over fat hub nodes and may expose fan as a first-class quality metric. ACT-R ships spreading, partial matching, and noise all OFF by default with exact-match as baseline — copy this conservative posture: exact deterministic retrieval as kernel default, similarity/stochastic exploration as opt-in pluggable policies.

findings.topic: ACT-R utility learning and production compilation: self-improvement with time-discounted rewards and trust-gated skill promotion
findings.summary: ACT-R 7 reference manual, utility module: conflict resolution picks the matching production with highest utility U; learning rule Ui(n) = Ui(n-1) + α[Ri(n) - Ui(n-1)], α (:alpha) default 0.2; effective reward Ri(n) is 'the reward value received... minus the time since that nth selection of production i' (built-in temporal discounting); utility noise :egs default 0. Production compilation module: 'composing two productions that fire in sequence into one new production', attempted for every consecutive pair; composability is governed by five buffer compilation types (goal, imaginal, retrieval, perceptual, motor) with a documented compatibility matrix (compilation.xls), plus a blocklist of ~13 disqualifying conditions (eval/bind conditions, buffer overwrites, multiple requests to same buffer, time-between-firings threshold, etc.). A newly compiled production starts at utility :nu default 0 (vs parents' learned utilities), so it must repeatedly be re-created and rewarded before it can out-compete its parents; if the new production is semantically equivalent to a previously compiled one, that one's utility is reinforced (effective reward = current :u of the first parent).
findings.designImplication: Blueprint for the OS-native self-improvement service: (1) credit assignment is kernel plumbing — rewards propagate to all procedures used since the last reward, discounted by elapsed time, with α=0.2 EMA as the default update; (2) skill compilation runs continuously and speculatively in the kernel (every adjacent action pair), but promotion is trust-gated: compiled skills start at utility 0 and must repeatedly prove out before winning selection over the deliberative path they shortcut — a built-in shadow-mode/canary discipline that prevents one bad compilation from hijacking behavior; (3) composability rules must be explicit and per-resource-type (ACT-R's five buffer compilation types → per-syscall-class composition rules: idempotent reads compose freely, external side-effecting actions compose conservatively or not at all). All three belong in the kernel as defaults; reward sources and the compiler itself are pluggable.

findings.topic: ACT-R declarative finsts: bounded recently-retrieved markers for iteration and loop prevention
findings.summary: ACT-R 7 reference manual: 'The declarative module maintains a record of the chunks which have been retrieved... through the use of a set of finsts (fingers of instantiation)'; retrieval requests can demand only chunks that have, or have not, been recently retrieved (:recently-retrieved t/nil). Finsts are scarce and expire: :declarative-num-finsts default 4; :declarative-finst-span default 3.0 seconds; when exhausted, the oldest finst is recycled. Soar's SMem offers the equivalent via multiple 'prohibit' command WMEs: 'This method can be used to iterate over all matching long-term identifiers.' ACT-R also has a speculative 'declarative-stuffing' mechanism (default off) that pushes the highest-activation chunk into the retrieval buffer unrequested.
findings.designImplication: The kernel memory-retrieval syscall needs a built-in 'exclude recently returned' facility — both architectures independently invented one because without it agents loop on the same top-ranked memory (an LLM-agent failure mode too: repeatedly retrieving the same RAG hit). Make the kernel keep a bounded, time-expiring set of recently-served memory IDs per agent (ACT-R: 4 markers / 3s — for an agent OS, size/TTL become per-session defaults scaled to task length) and expose retrieve-next/iterate semantics natively rather than forcing agents to track exclusion lists in their own context. Buffer 'stuffing' (proactive push of high-activation items) is the precedent for opt-in kernel-initiated memory suggestions — keep it off by default as ACT-R does.

findings.topic: 40-years survey: convergent memory taxonomy and concrete working-memory bounding strategies across 84 architectures
findings.summary: Kotseruba & Tsotsos, arXiv:1610.08602 (v3 2018): surveyed 84 implemented architectures (49 actively developed) with 900+ practical projects. The field converges on a standard memory taxonomy: long-term storage subdivided into semantic, procedural, episodic; short-term into sensory and working memory. Working memory bounding strategies observed: hard capacity limits ('3-6 objects in ARCADIA, 4 chunks in CHREST or up to 20 items in MDB'); time-based eviction ('4-9 seconds (EPIC) to 5 sec (MIDAS, CERA-CRANIUM) to tens of seconds (LIDA)'); activation-based gradual removal (CELTS threshold on decaying activation; Novamente atoms persist while building links/utility); and discard-on-task-switch (GLAIR). Sensory memory decay is 'tens of milliseconds' for visual data. Three dominant short-term-memory organizations: simple cache of current world state (most symbolic architectures), activation-weighted graph (ACT-R lineage; Soar, Sigma, NARS...), and blackboard — 'a shared repository of goals, problems and partial results which can be accessed and modified by the modules running in parallel' (LIDA, Ymir, PolyScheme, PRS...).
findings.designImplication: Strong empirical license for the OS's DEFAULT tiered memory layout: ship exactly the five-way split (working + sensory/ingest buffer; semantic + episodic + procedural long-term stores) as the kernel default, because 84 independent systems converged on it — deviation needs justification, the default does not. Eviction policy should be a typed kernel interface with the three observed strategies as built-in options (capacity-bounded, TTL-bounded, activation-thresholded), defaulting to activation-thresholded for working memory per the most-validated lineage. For one-or-many agents per session, the blackboard pattern is the proven structure: implement multi-agent sessions as a shared kernel working-memory segment with parallel module/agent writers — IPC via shared cognitive state rather than message queues alone.

openQuestions: Timescale rescaling: ACT-R/Soar constants (50ms cycle, d=0.5 decay over seconds, 3s finst span, 4-9s WM eviction) are calibrated to human cognition. LLM-agent steps take 1-60s and sessions span weeks — should the kernel define decay in wall-clock time, in decision-cycle counts, or in token/cost units, and what recalibrated defaults are defensible?

Episodic compaction: Soar EpMem explicitly implements no forgetting and degrades to linear scan on adversarial cues. What default compaction/summarization/indexing tier should an agent OS ship for unbounded episodic journals, and is summarization (lossy) acceptable in the canonical kernel journal or only in derived views?

Chunking for LLM traces: Soar's chunk safety rests on exact symbolic dependency backtracing (justifications, GDS). For natural-language reasoning chains, what provenance granularity (tool-call boundaries? memory-read boundaries? token attribution?) is sufficient for the kernel skill compiler to produce safe, generalizable compiled skills?

Serialization scope: both architectures serialize at one production/operator per cycle per cognitive agent. In a multi-agent session sharing a blackboard working memory, should the kernel serialize per-agent (parallel agents, transactional WM) or per-shared-resource, and what consistency model does the Soar i-support auto-retraction semantics imply under concurrent writers?

Register width: ACT-R's one-chunk-per-buffer is a human calibration. For context-window-budgeted LLM agents, what is the right default register set (count, per-register token budget) and should the kernel enforce hard caps (ARCADIA-style 3-6 items) or soft activation thresholds?

Utility vs activation unification: ACT-R uses utility (reward-driven) for procedure selection and activation (usage-driven) for declarative retrieval as separate subsymbolic systems. Should the OS keep two ranking planes (skill trust vs memory salience) or unify them into one credit/salience ledger?
